annotations.label: Optimal substructure

anchor.snippet: if a problem can be solved optimally by breaking it into sub-problems and then recursively finding the optimal solutions to the sub-problems, then it is said to have optimal substructure

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general formalization of the dynamic-programming concept of optimal substructure.

annotations.label: Bellman equation (lead)

anchor.snippet: In the optimization literature this relationship is called the Bellman equation

annotations.note: No declaration referencing the Bellman equation was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Value functions sequence

anchor.snippet: This is done by defining a sequence of value functions

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize value functions of a sequential optimization problem.

annotations.label: Terminal value function

anchor.snippet: The definition of V n ( y ) is the value obtained in state y at the last time n

annotations.note: Mathlib has no terminal value function definition for DP problems.

annotations.label: Backward recursion for value functions

anchor.snippet: can be found by working backwards, using a recursive relationship called the Bellman equation

annotations.note: No formalization of the backward induction principle for Bellman-style recursions exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bellman recursion step

anchor.snippet: at any state y is calculated from V i by maximizing a simple function (usually the sum) of the gain from a decision at time

annotations.note: The Bellman recursion step is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Optimal value at initial state

anchor.snippet: at the initial state of the system is the value of the optimal solution

annotations.note: No corresponding statement linking initial-state value to optimal cost is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Optimal control problem

anchor.snippet: a typical problem is to find an admissible control

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize optimal control problems.

annotations.label: Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman equation

anchor.snippet: a partial differential equation known as the Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman equation

annotations.note: No HJB equation is present in Mathlib (grep for Hamilton-Jacobi returned no files).

annotations.label: Discrete Bellman equation

anchor.snippet: This functional equation is known as the Bellman equation , which can be solved for an exact solution of the discrete approximation of the optimization equation

annotations.note: Discrete Bellman functional equation not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain economic models such as Ramsey's optimal saving problem.

anchor.section: Example from economics: Ramsey's problem of optimal saving
anchor.snippet: we define a sequence of value functions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no value-function sequence for the Ramsey problem.

annotations.label: Bellman equation for Ramsey

anchor.section: Example from economics: Ramsey's problem of optimal saving
anchor.snippet: The value of any quantity of capital at any previous time can be calculated by backward induction using the Bellman equation

annotations.note: Bellman equation specialized to Ramsey is not in Mathlib.

anchor.section: Example from economics: Ramsey's problem of optimal saving
anchor.snippet: Working backwards, it can be shown that the value function at time

annotations.note: Closed-form value function for Ramsey is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Optimal consumption rule

anchor.section: Example from economics: Ramsey's problem of optimal saving
anchor.snippet: We see that it is optimal to consume a larger fraction of current wealth as one gets older

annotations.note: Optimal consumption rule from the Ramsey problem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Attributes for DP applicability

anchor.snippet: There are two key attributes that a problem must have in order for dynamic programming to be applicable: optimal substructure and overlapping sub-problems

annotations.note: These informal CS prerequisites are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Optimal substructure definition

anchor.snippet: Optimal substructure means that the solution to a given optimization problem can be obtained by the combination of optimal solutions to its sub-problems

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks a general definition of optimal substructure.

annotations.label: Shortest path optimal substructure

anchor.snippet: the shortest path p from a vertex u to a vertex v exhibits optimal substructure

annotations.note: Mathlib has no shortest-path framework formalizing this substructure property.

annotations.label: Overlapping sub-problems

anchor.snippet: Overlapping sub-problems means that the space of sub-problems must be small

annotations.note: No formal notion of overlapping sub-problems is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fibonacci overlapping subproblems

anchor.snippet: consider the recursive formulation for generating the Fibonacci sequence

annotations.note: `Nat.fib` defines the Fibonacci sequence with the standard recurrence, but the DP/overlap analysis itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Top-down memoization

anchor.snippet: Top-down approach : This is the direct fall-out of the recursive formulation of any problem

annotations.note: Memoization is an algorithmic technique not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bottom-up approach

anchor.snippet: Bottom-up approach : Once we formulate the solution to a problem recursively as in terms of its sub-problems

annotations.note: Bottom-up tabulation is not a formal Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Dijkstra as DP

anchor.section: Dijkstra's algorithm for the shortest path problem
anchor.snippet: Dijkstra's algorithm for the shortest path problem is a successive approximation scheme that solves the dynamic programming functional equation for the shortest path problem by the Reaching method

annotations.note: Dijkstra's algorithm is not present in Mathlib (grep returned no files).

annotations.label: Principle of optimality for shortest paths

anchor.section: Dijkstra's algorithm for the shortest path problem
anchor.snippet: is a paraphrasing of Bellman's famous Principle of Optimality in the context of the shortest path problem

annotations.note: Bellman's principle of optimality is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Naive Fibonacci call tree

anchor.snippet: Notice that if we call, say, fib(5) , we produce a call tree that calls the function on the same value many different times

annotations.note: Call-tree analysis of recursive Fibonacci is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Memoized Fibonacci complexity

anchor.snippet: The resulting function requires only

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize time-complexity of memoized Fibonacci.

annotations.label: Memoization

anchor.snippet: This technique of saving values that have already been calculated is called memoization

annotations.note: Memoization is not a Mathlib notion.

annotations.label: Bottom-up Fibonacci complexity

anchor.snippet: This method also uses O( n ) time since it contains a loop that repeats

annotations.note: No complexity claim about iterative Fibonacci is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Balanced 0–1 matrix problem

anchor.snippet: Consider the problem of assigning values, either zero or one, to the positions of an n × n matrix

annotations.note: The balanced 0–1 matrix counting problem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: DP counts balanced matrices

anchor.snippet: Dynamic programming makes it possible to count the number of solutions without visiting them all

annotations.note: No formal counting via DP for this problem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Checkerboard shortest path

anchor.snippet: Consider a checkerboard with n × n squares and a cost function

annotations.note: Checkerboard minimum-cost problem not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Minimum cost function q(i,j)

anchor.snippet: the minimum cost to reach square

annotations.note: No checkerboard cost function definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recurrence for q(i,j)

anchor.snippet: is equal to the minimum cost to get to any of the three squares below it

annotations.note: Checkerboard recurrence is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sequence alignment problem

anchor.snippet: the problem consists of transforming one sequence into another using edit operations that replace, insert, or remove an element

annotations.note: Edit distance / sequence alignment is not present in Mathlib (no Levenshtein decl found).

annotations.label: Recursive alignment

anchor.snippet: The problem can be stated naturally as a recursion

annotations.note: No recursive alignment formulation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matrix chain multiplication

anchor.snippet: Matrix chain multiplication is a well-known example that demonstrates utility of dynamic programming

annotations.note: Matrix chain multiplication optimization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matrix multiplication cost

anchor.snippet: If matrix A has dimensions m×n and matrix B has dimensions n×q, then matrix C=A×B will have dimensions m×q, and will require m*n*q scalar multiplications

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Matrix.mul` but does not formalize scalar-multiplication counts.

annotations.label: Parenthesization comparison

anchor.snippet: Let us assume that m = 10, n = 100, p = 10 and s = 1000

annotations.note: The illustrative parenthesization comparison is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: m[i,j] minimum multiplications

anchor.snippet: Let's call m[i,j] the minimum number of scalar multiplications needed to multiply a chain of matrices from matrix i to matrix j

annotations.note: The m[i,j] DP table for matrix chain order is not formalized in Mathlib.
